chunks.text: On the battlefield, there is a fear that no movie can convey.
An armed troop advances through the forest.
Everyone holds their breath.
Suddenly, from the front, the sound of a blast echoed.
The commander gave orders in a low voice.
" Scatter.
Everyone, drop to the ground.
Bullet flew between the trees.
Combat had begun.
Beside a young soldier, a comrade fell.
A bloody scene spread before them.
Minutes later, the shooting stopped, and the area was wrapped in an uncanny silence.
The soldiers, trembling, rose to their feet.
Who had survived, who had been lost?
The tragedy of war is the accumulation of such moments.
Even the distinction between friend and enemy is sometimes lost in confusion.
Soldiers who return from the battlefield are haunted by those memories for life.
Some are driven to PTSD, depression, or suicide.
The wounds of war remain deep long after the shooting has stopped.
" There are no winners in war," a veteran once said.
Both victory and defeat give birth to the same grief in the end.
Will the day ever come when humanity can truly abolish war?
